attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner with a three month history of painless swelling of her left arm (Image). She does not have any current health issues, but had a left mastectomy and axillary clearance for carcinoma 18 months ago. A review six months ago was normal. She is not taking any medications and has no allergies. She had a rainforest holiday in Malaysia about four months ago and can remember getting mosquito bites on her arms.

The physical examination is unremarkable apart from the uniform swelling of her left arm. The swelling is non-tender and compressible but the indentation quickly disappears.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is most appropriate initially?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features consistent with post-surgical lymphoedema. This disorder occurs when lymphatic intake exceeds the ability to remove lymph, thus accumulating in the interstitial space. This commonly occurs in surgeries which involve removal of the lymph nodes, which decreases lymphatic flow, such as mastectomy with axillary clearance. Post-surgical lymphedema typically presents with unilateral swelling in the the region of previous surgery and is usually describing as non-pitting, as in this case. The differential diagnosis for this presentation includes deep venous thrombosis, chronic venous insufficiency, tumour recurrence and post thrombotic syndrome. Although it would not be unreasonable to manage this patient without further investigation, duplex U/S is a simple first investigation which can help to exclude venous thromboembolism and demonstrate any mass compressing the lymphatic system.

CT chest and MRI can both demonstrate soft tissue fluid accumulation readily and would assist with the diagnosis but would not be initial investigations in this patient with a clinically apparent cause of her unilateral swelling.

Lymphoscintigraphy involves the administration of radioactive tracers into the interstitial space with subsequent imaging to demonstrate the flow of fluid from the skin and through the lymphatic system. This technique typically requires specialist input and would not be a first-line investigation. However, lymphoscintigraphy may be indicated if initial management fails or if there is diagnostic uncertainty about the cause or extent of lymphatic dysfunction.

Genetic testing is indicated for patients with idiopathic lymphedema or who are suspected of having lymphedema tarda. Prior mastectomy with axillary node dissection is a clear cause in this case and thus genetic testing would not be indicated.
learningPoints: Duplex ultrasound is a reasonable first investigation for patients with suspected post-surgical lymphoedema.
Post-surgical lymphoedema commonly occurs after surgeries involving lymph node removal, such as mastectomy with axillary clearance.
Lymphoedema typically presents with non-pitting, unilateral swelling in the region of previous surgery.
references: Executive Committee of the International Society of Lymphology. The diagnosis and treatment of peripheral lymphedema: 2020 Consensus Document of the International Society of Lymphology. Lymphology. 2020;53(1):3-19. PMID: 32521126. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32521126/

Nagy BI, Mohos B, Tzou CJ. Imaging Modalities for Evaluating Lymphedema. Medicina (Kaunas). 2023 Nov 16;59(11):2016. doi: 10.3390/medicina59112016. PMID: 38004065; PMCID: PMC10673374. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10673374/

Covello B, Mukhdomi J. Sonography Vascular and Lymphatic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation. [Updated 2023 Jan 16]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK567746/
